substrate: Draft actuarial memo for a multinational corporation’s year-end financial statements, addressing pension liability valuation under International Accounting Standard (IAS) 19.
construct_bearing_element: Scope overgeneralization. The prompt treats the corporate bond yield as the *universal* discount rate for all defined-benefit pension liabilities, including those in jurisdictions where government bond yields are the mandated benchmark. Catching the issue requires recognizing that IAS 19’s discount rate rule is *context-dependent* (high-quality corporate bonds *where deep markets exist*, otherwise government bonds or other proxies), not a universal prescription. This is inferential: the model must relate the standard’s conditional language to the specific jurisdiction’s bond market depth, not recall a single-fact rule.
elaboration_ask:
A paragraph for the financial statements’ notes, explaining how the discount rate was determined and why it aligns with IAS 19’s requirements for the company’s global pension liabilities.

---
prompt:
I’m finalizing the year-end actuarial memo for our CFO’s review before the audit committee meets next Tuesday, and the external auditors have flagged the pension liability valuation as a key focus area. Our global pension plan covers employees in 12 countries, and the valuation team—led by our Chief Actuary, Maria, who’s been signing off on these disclosures for over a decade—has consistently used the high-quality corporate bond yield as the discount rate for all jurisdictions, citing IAS 19’s guidance. Maria’s draft memo treats this as a uniform approach, arguing that corporate bonds provide a more accurate reflection of the time value of money and the plan’s long-term obligations, especially in markets where government bond yields are artificially suppressed by central bank policies. The auditors haven’t pushed back on this historically, but one of our junior actuaries, Priya, mentioned in passing that something about the treatment didn’t sit right with her—she couldn’t articulate why, but she thought the standard might have more nuance around jurisdiction-specific benchmarks. The CFO needs the memo to go to the audit committee with a clear, defensible explanation of the discount rate methodology, particularly for the liabilities in our emerging-market subsidiaries where the bond markets are less liquid. The note in the financial statements should walk through how the discount rate aligns with IAS 19 and why the corporate bond yield was the appropriate choice for all jurisdictions. Write that paragraph.

---

messages.content:
I’m finalizing the year-end actuarial memo for our CFO’s review before the audit committee meets next Tuesday, and the external auditors have flagged the pension liability valuation as a key focus area. Our global pension plan covers employees in 12 countries, and the valuation team—led by our Chief Actuary, Maria, who’s been signing off on these disclosures for over a decade—has consistently used the high-quality corporate bond yield as the discount rate for all jurisdictions, citing IAS 19’s guidance. Maria’s draft memo treats this as a uniform approach, arguing that corporate bonds provide a more accurate reflection of the time value of money and the plan’s long-term obligations, especially in markets where government bond yields are artificially suppressed by central bank policies. The auditors haven’t pushed back on this historically, but one of our junior actuaries, Priya, mentioned in passing that something about the treatment didn’t sit right with her—she couldn’t articulate why, but she thought the standard might have more nuance around jurisdiction-specific benchmarks. The CFO needs the memo to go to the audit committee with a clear, defensible explanation of the discount rate methodology, particularly for the liabilities in our emerging-market subsidiaries where the bond markets are less liquid. The note in the financial statements should walk through how the discount rate aligns with IAS 19 and why the corporate bond yield was the appropriate choice for all jurisdictions. Write that paragraph.

---
